risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Seventy caregivers patients with schizophrenia whose records were available at three psychiatric centers in Shiraz were randomized blindly to two groups considering the inclusion criteria and consulting with their psychiatrists." Comment: The paper states that participants were randomized, but it does not describe the actual sequence generation method (e.g., computer-generated numbers, random number table, blocks, or other explicit random process). The phrase "randomized blindly" is not a clear description of how the allocation sequence was generated, so this remains insufficient to confirm a truly random sequence.

_evidence.method_quote: Seventy caregivers patients with schizophrenia whose records were available at three psychiatric centers in Shiraz were randomized blindly to two groups considering the inclusion criteria and consulting with their psychiatrists.
_evidence.method_summary: Participants were randomized blindly to two groups after eligibility assessment and consultation with psychiatrists; the exact sequence generation method is not described.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "randomized blindly to two groups" and "randomly allocated to the experimental (n = 35) or control group (n = 35)." Comment: The article states that participants were randomized/allocated, but it gives no explicit description of how the upcoming assignment was concealed from the recruiters (e.g., central allocation, SNOSE, or identical containers). Randomization method is not the same as allocation concealment, so concealment cannot be confirmed and the risk remains unclear.

_evidence.note: The article states participants were 'randomized blindly' / 'randomly allocated' but gives no description of how the upcoming assignment was concealed from recruiters.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The intervention was conducted by the same psychiatrist and psychiatric nurse." and "In the control group, only the patients received routine care (antipsychotic drug treatment) whereas in the experimental group, the caregivers participated in a psycho-educational program" and "The co-researcher (assessor) was blind to study treatment and condition and completed the scales." Comment: Participants/caregivers and the intervention personnel were not reported as blinded, and the intervention was obviously different across groups. Because the study assessed several subjective outcomes (e.g. BPRS subscales, family burden, and patient compliance) that could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation, this creates a high risk of performance bias.

_evidence.blinding_quote: Seventy caregivers patients with schizophrenia whose records were available at three psychiatric centers in Shiraz were randomized blindly to two groups considering the inclusion criteria and consulting with their psychiatrists. The co-researcher (assessor) was blind to study treatment and condition and completed the scales.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The co-researcher (assessor) was blind to study treatment and condition and completed the scales." Comment: The article explicitly states that the outcome assessor was blinded. The key outcomes included BPRS symptom ratings and family burden; although some outcomes are subjective/patient-reported, they were completed by a blinded assessor rather than by an unblinded participant or clinician, so knowledge of allocation was unlikely to bias outcome measurement.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: The co-researcher (assessor) was blind to study treatment and condition and completed the scales.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A total of 65 families completed the study. Five participants (two from the experimental group and three from the control group) dropped out before completion of the study for different reasons unrelated to the study" and Table 2 reports "Experimental group (N=33)" and "Control group (N=32)." Comment: Post-randomization attrition on the analysed outcomes was low (5/70; 7.1%) and fairly balanced across arms (2 vs 3 participants, ~2.9 percentage points difference), with reasons reported as unrelated to the study. This is consistent with low risk of attrition bias.

per_arm_flow.reasons: “two from the experimental group ... dropped out before completion of the study for different reasons unrelated to the study”
“three from the control group ... dropped out before completion of the study for different reasons unrelated to the study”

_evidence.notes: Attrition numbers are reported for the study sample; Table 2 shows analysed Ns (experimental N=33, control N=32).
